name: Dynamic Programming
tagline: Solve sequential decision problems by working BACKWARD from the final stage. The principle of optimality says the optimal tail policy is itself optimal -- so memoize from the end.

blocks.text: Dynamic programming solves sequential optimization problems by exploiting the principle of optimality: an optimal policy is optimal at every state it reaches. The recipe is BACKWARD: J_N(x) = terminal value; J_k(x) = max over decisions u of E[g_k(x, u, w) + J_{k+1}(f(x, u, w))]. Solve from the final stage backward to stage 0.

DP shines on stopping problems (when to roll the die, when to draw a card) and replicating-portfolio problems (the World Series betting problem is structurally identical to pricing a binomial-tree option). The 'value of continuing' is computed against the 'value of stopping,' and the optimal action is whichever is larger.

blocks.label: Mental model
blocks.text: Define J(x) = optimal value at state x. Write J recursively from the boundary backward. The optimal action at x is the argmax in the recursion. This is option-pricing in disguise: 'continuation value vs intrinsic value' is the same as 'expected DP value vs immediate payoff.'

whenToUse: Optimal stopping problems (when to sell, when to roll again).
Multi-stage games where each stage has finite decisions.
Problems with a natural state space and additive payoffs.
Anywhere 'continuation value vs immediate payoff' is the right framing.
recipe: Identify the state space and the terminal stage with known values.
Write the recursion J_k(x) = max over u of E[immediate + J_{k+1}(next state)].
Solve backward from the terminal stage.
Read off the OPTIMAL DECISION at each state from the argmax in the recursion.
For continuous-state problems, often there's a clean THRESHOLD strategy (stop above some level, continue below).
commonPitfalls: Working FORWARD instead of backward -- DP requires the future to be solved before the present.
Forgetting that the value function is the OPTIMAL value, not the value under a fixed strategy.
Conflating the DP value (continuous) with the optimal action (discrete decision rule).
Failing to recognize a DP problem disguised as a probability problem.
